_description: SwitchLingua 2.0 Stage 1 Layer 2: Group-level robust statistics for es-en (Spanglish)
_note: All statistics are group-level aggregates, not individual-level. Spanglish has moderate-high CMI. US Latino communities tend to show higher mixing than Latin America.

cmi_distribution._description: Overall CMI distribution (normalized to [0,1]). Spanglish shows moderate-high mixing in US communities.
cmi_distribution.source: Bangor Miami corpus analysis; Gamback & Das (2014) CMI definition

by_region._note: US-based speakers tend to mix more than Latin America-based speakers

us_latino.note: Higher mixing in US Latino communities, especially 2nd+ generation

latin_america_urban.note: Lower mixing in Latin American cities, mainly insertional

by_topic._note: Topic-CMI associations based on literature trends, not exact values

daily_life.note: Daily topics still show moderate mixing in US communities

food.note: Food topics lean toward Spanish, but English brand names appear

emotion.note: Emotional expression tends toward L1 Spanish

entertainment.note: Entertainment heavily influenced by English media

switch_type_distribution._description: Switch type distribution based on Spanglish corpus studies
switch_type_distribution.source: Poplack (1980); Deuchar et al. (2014); Bangor Miami corpus

intra_sentential.note: Intra-sentential switching is dominant, consistent with dense bilingual mixing

inter_sentential.note: Inter-sentential switching is common, especially in more formal settings

tag_switching.note: Tag switching includes discourse markers, fillers, and interjections

switch_position_distribution._description: Syntactic position distribution of switch points
switch_position_distribution.source: Poplack (1980) Equivalence & Free Morpheme Constraints; Bangor Miami analysis

NP_boundary.note: Noun phrase position most common, consistent with Insertion model

VP_boundary.note: Verb phrase boundary; Spanish verb + English complement common

CP_boundary.note: Clause/sentence boundary, corresponding to Alternation model

discourse_marker.note: Discourse markers (you know, o sea, like, bueno)

high_frequency_L2_elements.source: Bangor Miami corpus; LINCE dataset; community observations
L2_fixed_expressions_top30: at the end of the day

common_switch_triggers._note: Common context patterns that trigger switching

archetype_distribution_estimate._description: Estimated archetype proportions in real Spanglish data
archetype_distribution_estimate._method: Based on CMI distribution and switch type patterns from Bangor Miami and Poplack's studies

ARC_01_insertional.reasoning: CMI < 0.25 with primarily intra-sentential switches; common in Latin America-based speakers

ARC_02_alternational.reasoning: CMI 0.20-0.40 with higher inter-sentential proportion; common in balanced bilinguals

ARC_03_dense_mixer.reasoning: CMI > 0.40; more common in US-born Latinos than in zh-en, reflecting deeper bilingual integration

ARC_04_pragmatic.reasoning: Strategic switching tied to pragmatic functions; common in professional/media contexts

ARC_05_reluctant.reasoning: CMI < 0.10; less common than in zh-en as Spanglish communities generally mix more

ARC_06_accommodation.reasoning: Accommodation-driven switching; common in mixed-generation or cross-regional interactions

validation_notes.confidence: Moderate — based on published corpus statistics and sociolinguistic literature
limitations: Bangor Miami corpus represents a specific Miami community; may not generalize to all Spanglish varieties
Regional variation (Chicano, Nuyorican, Miami Cuban, etc.) is substantial
LINCE dataset includes social media text which may differ from spoken patterns
Dense mixing (ARC_03) proportion is higher in Spanglish than zh-en, reflecting deeper community bilingualism
next_steps: Incorporate speaker-level statistics from Bangor Miami corpus if raw data is accessible
Validate with additional corpora (e.g., CALLHOME Spanish-English)
Account for generational differences (1st gen vs. 2nd gen vs. 3rd gen speakers)
